evidence.text: RUN YOUR WASHING MACHINE ONLY WHEN IT IS FULL TO PRESERVE THE ENVIRONMENT

editableNewDialogue.text: USE LED BULBS INSTEAD OF INCANDESCENT BULBS TO PRESERVE THE ENVIRONMENT

Using LED bulbs instead of incandescent bulbs helps preserve the environment because LEDs use much less electricity for the same amount of light and typically last much longer. That reduces power-plant emissions and lowers waste from frequent bulb replacements.

To maximize the benefit:
- Choose the right brightness (look at lumens, not watts).
- Pick warm/cool color temperature based on the space.
- Turn lights off when not needed, even with efficient bulbs.

As a general example (not about you), replacing a single 60W incandescent used a few hours a day with a ~9W LED can save a noticeable amount of energy over a year.
UNPLUG CHARGERS WHEN NOT IN USE

Unplugging chargers when they’re not in use can reduce “standby” or “vampire” power draw, which saves electricity and indirectly reduces greenhouse-gas emissions. Some chargers draw very little when idle, but unplugging is still a simple way to cut unnecessary consumption.

If unplugging is inconvenient, a power strip with an on/off switch can make it easier.

Fake but relevant example: many energy agencies advise hunting for standby loads because, across an entire household, small always-on draws can add up over time.
AIR-DRY CLOTHES INSTEAD OF USING A DRYER

Air-drying clothes instead of using a dryer helps preserve the environment by cutting one of the more energy-intensive steps in laundry. It can also be gentler on fabrics, which may extend clothing life.

If space or weather is an issue, alternatives include:
- Using an indoor drying rack near good ventilation
- Spinning clothes well to reduce drying time
- Using the dryer only to finish (short cycle) rather than fully dry

Unrelated fact: in some cities, outdoor clotheslines are encouraged through “right to dry” policies to support energy conservation.
I’ve started keeping the TV and phone chargers on one switchable power strip, so at night I just flick it off instead of leaving everything on standby.

That’s a practical way to cut “phantom” electricity use. A switchable power strip makes it easy to shut down multiple devices at once, especially overnight or when you’re out, while still keeping your setup convenient.
About that line earlier—I'm not saying public transport is better than cycling. If I can, I prefer cycling because it has basically zero emissions.

Got it—so you *don’t* prefer public transportation over cycling for environmental reasons. You actually prefer cycling when it’s feasible, since it produces no direct emissions, and you’d use public transport mainly when cycling isn’t practical (distance, safety, weather, or time).
Yes, exactly. Cycling first when possible; public transportation only as a backup.
